params.text:
Assessing the risk before you go

A good lone-working risk assessment looks at the task, the person, the environment and the journey, and then decides what controls are needed. Your employer carries out this assessment, but you are a vital source of up-to-date information. If something changes at a property, or a person's behaviour becomes unpredictable, tell your manager so the assessment can be reviewed.

Some tasks should never be done alone. For example, certain moving and handling operations may need two workers. If an assessment says a visit needs two people, it must not be attempted by one.

  • Consider the person's history, health and any known triggers.
  • Consider the property: access, lighting, pets, other people present.
  • Consider the journey: route, timing, parking and lone travel after dark.
  • Match the control measures to the level of risk, and review them when things change.

params.question: A risk assessment says a moving and handling task needs two carers, but your colleague has called in sick. What should you do?

answers.text: Do not attempt it alone. Contact your manager so cover can be arranged or the visit rescheduled

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. If an assessment specifies two workers, doing it alone risks injury to you and the person. Escalate instead.

answers.text: Attempt it on your own to avoid letting the person down

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Attempting a two-person task alone puts both you and the person at serious risk of injury. Never override the assessment.

answers.text: Ask the person's relative to help you lift, whatever their ability

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. An untrained relative may be unable to help safely and could be injured. The safe route is to contact your manager.

answers.text: Skip the task entirely and say nothing

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Missing essential care without reporting it can leave the person at risk. Always escalate so a safe solution is found.
